worth_it_explanation: On-target percentage is the metric media buyers use to confirm that impressions landed on the intended demographic, not just that they were served. A campaign that hit its impression goal but delivered half of them outside the target age and gender did not perform as planned, and the shortfall has real budget and negotiation consequences. These checks confirm that the on-target figure is reviewed with enough context to be acted on, that a review cadence exists during the flight rather than only at the end, and that the measured rate is read alongside the on-target percentage so a high figure on a thin measurement base is not mistaken for strong delivery.
intro: Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) measures deduplicated unique audience, reach, frequency, and on-target percentage across desktop, mobile, and connected TV (CTV) using a panel-plus-census methodology. On-target percentage is the share of measured impressions that reached the campaign's intended demographic as defined in the media plan. It is the primary signal for whether bought impressions matched the purchased audience. Reviewing it accurately requires knowing not only what the percentage is but how many impressions Comscore actually measured, whether the result met the plan's goal or any guaranteed threshold, and whether the team had an opportunity to correct underperformance while the campaign was still running. These checks audit those three conditions.

audit_checks.title: On-target percentage is below the media plan goal and the shortfall is not investigated or corrected
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A campaign that delivered impressions below the contracted or planned on-target threshold spent budget on audience it did not intend to reach. When the gap is not investigated, the cause remains unknown, the publisher or platform is not held to a guarantee, and the same pattern repeats on the next flight.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) reports on-target percentage as the share of measured impressions confirmed to have reached the campaign's intended demographic, typically defined by age, gender, and sometimes geography. Most media plans set an expected on-target percentage based on the audience and placement mix. Some direct buys include a contractual guarantee. When the CCR report shows a result below that benchmark, two separate questions arise: why did delivery miss, and what remedy is available.

Common causes of a below-goal on-target percentage include demographic targeting that was too broad at the line item or placement level, publisher inventory that skewed outside the target segment, and measurement methodology differences between the ad server and Comscore that affected which impressions Comscore was able to classify. Identifying the cause requires comparing the on-target percentage across placements, publishers, and devices to see whether the miss was concentrated in one area or spread across the buy.

Where a contractual guarantee exists, a documented shortfall is the basis for a make-good conversation with the publisher. Where no guarantee exists, the shortfall is still useful for evaluating which placements to reduce or remove on future flights. In either case, the shortfall is only actionable if it is noticed and traced to a cause.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The CCR report shows an on-target percentage below the figure in the media plan or insertion order (IO), and there is no record of the gap being reviewed
  • The post-campaign summary reports total impressions and reach without noting whether on-target delivery met the plan goal
  • The campaign included a publisher guarantee on on-target delivery but no comparison of the CCR result against the guaranteed threshold was documented
You may want to compare the CCR on-target percentage to the plan benchmark and, if a gap exists, trace it to the placement or publisher level before closing the campaign.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a brand awareness campaign targeting a specific age and gender demographic ahead of a promotional period, an on-target miss means impressions were delivered to people outside the target who are less likely to convert during the promotion. You may want to confirm the CCR on-target percentage was compared against the plan goal and that any shortfall was documented before the campaign closed.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser buying programmatic display to reach a defined professional or demographic audience, an on-target miss reduces the quality of the primed audience entering the next retargeting phase. You may want to confirm the CCR on-target figure was reviewed against the plan goal and that any placement-level shortfall was investigated.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore Campaign Ratings methodology documentation, including how on-target percentage is calculated and how to interpret a shortfall against a plan benchmark, is available through Comscore's client portal. Contact your Comscore account representative to access the CCR reporting guide.

investigate.intro: Review the CCR post-campaign or in-flight report for on-target percentage, compare it against the plan goal or IO guarantee, and trace any gap to the placement or publisher level.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Open the CCR report for the campaign. Locate the on-target percentage column for the primary demographic target (e.g., Adults 25-54).
Compare the reported on-target percentage to the figure stated in the media plan or IO. Note the difference in percentage points.
If the report shows placement-level or publisher-level breakdowns, identify which placements or publishers contributed the most to off-target delivery.
Check whether the IO or any publisher agreement included a guaranteed on-target percentage. If so, note whether the CCR result fell below that threshold.
Document the gap and the placement breakdown. This is the record needed to initiate a make-good conversation or to adjust the next flight's placement mix.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the CCR on-target percentage and the plan goal or IO guarantee. If the CCR result meets or exceeds the goal, the check passes. If the CCR result falls below the goal or guarantee, the check is a fail, recorded with the gap in percentage points, the placements or publishers where the miss was concentrated, and whether a make-good or adjustment was documented.
evaluate.fallback_message: Enter the CCR on-target percentage and the plan goal or IO guarantee above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Comscore Campaign Ratings on-target percentage for this campaign fell below the media plan goal, and the shortfall was not investigated or documented. Impressions delivered outside the intended demographic represent budget that did not reach the purchased audience. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the CCR on-target figure to the plan benchmark at the placement and publisher level, documenting the gap, and using that breakdown to initiate a make-good where a guarantee applies or to remove underperforming placements from future flights on {{site_name}}.

audit_checks.title: On-target performance is only reviewed at the end of the campaign with no in-flight checkpoint
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An on-target problem discovered after a campaign ends cannot be corrected. A mid-flight review creates an opportunity to shift budget away from underperforming placements or request a publisher adjustment before the remainder of the budget is spent.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings can produce reports during a flight as well as at the end of it. In-flight CCR data shows the on-target percentage accumulated to date, which lets the team see whether delivery is tracking toward the plan goal while there is still budget to reallocate.

Without an in-flight checkpoint, the first time the team sees the on-target percentage is in the post-campaign report. At that point, the budget has been spent. If on-target delivery was running low from the first week, that information was available but not reviewed, and every impression served after that point continued at the same below-goal rate.

A mid-flight review does not require a formal report or a lengthy process. It requires pulling the CCR pacing data, comparing the on-target percentage to the plan goal, and making a note of whether any placement or publisher adjustment is warranted. For campaigns with a publisher guarantee, a mid-flight check also creates the opportunity to notify the publisher of a pacing problem before the campaign ends, which is almost always easier to resolve than a post-campaign dispute.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The campaign ran for four weeks or longer but the CCR report was only reviewed at the end
  • There is no record of a mid-flight pull of the CCR on-target figure in the campaign notes or buy sheet
  • The team's process for reviewing Comscore data is to wait for the final campaign report rather than to pull in-flight data on a set schedule
You may want to confirm that in-flight CCR data is pulled and reviewed at least once for campaigns longer than two weeks, and that the review cadence is documented in the campaign plan.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a four-week brand campaign before a peak sales period, a mid-flight CCR review at the two-week mark gives the team time to shift budget to higher-performing placements before the most valuable impressions of the flight are delivered. You may want to confirm the campaign plan includes a scheduled in-flight CCR review.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running a multi-week awareness campaign, an in-flight CCR check confirms whether the audience being primed for the retargeting phase actually matches the intended demographic. You may want to confirm there is a scheduled point during the flight when the on-target percentage is reviewed and a decision about placement adjustments is documented.

learn_more.source_title: Third-party measurement on YouTube in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the campaign plan includes a scheduled in-flight CCR review, and whether any in-flight CCR data was pulled and acted on during the flight.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Ask the team whether the campaign plan included a scheduled in-flight CCR data pull. Check the buy sheet or campaign notes for a reference to a mid-flight review date.
Open the CCR report and check whether there is a version or export dated during the campaign flight, distinct from the final post-campaign report.
If an in-flight pull exists, confirm whether any placement or budget adjustment was made in response to the on-target data from that pull.
If no in-flight pull exists, note the campaign duration and the date the first CCR review took place relative to the campaign end date.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the campaign included a scheduled in-flight CCR review and whether any in-flight data was pulled. A campaign of four weeks or longer with a documented in-flight CCR review passes. A campaign of four weeks or longer with no in-flight CCR pull is a fail, recorded with the campaign duration and the date of the first CCR review relative to the campaign end.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign duration, the date of the first CCR review, and whether any in-flight data pull was documented above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that Comscore Campaign Ratings data for campaigns on {{site_name}} is reviewed only after the campaign ends, with no in-flight checkpoint during the flight. On-target delivery that is running below goal cannot be corrected once the budget is spent. {{voice_recommend}} adding a scheduled in-flight CCR review to the campaign plan for any flight longer than two weeks, and documenting whether a placement or budget adjustment was made based on the in-flight on-target data, so the review is part of the standard campaign management process rather than an optional post-mortem step.

audit_checks.title: On-target percentage is read without the measured rate, so a high figure on a low-coverage base is mistaken for strong delivery
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A 90% on-target percentage on 5% measured impressions describes a very different campaign than 90% on-target on 80% measured impressions. Reading only the percentage without the coverage context produces a misleading picture of delivery quality and can cause the team to declare success on a flight that had a significant measurement gap.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings uses a panel-plus-census methodology. The census layer collects impression counts from participating publishers and platforms. The panel layer classifies the demographic profile of users who saw those impressions. The measured rate, sometimes called the coverage rate, is the share of total served impressions that Comscore was able to classify demographically. Impressions that were not measured, meaning Comscore could not match them to a demographic profile, are not included in the on-target percentage calculation.

This creates a meaningful interpretation question. An on-target percentage of 85% looks strong in isolation. If the measured rate was 20%, that 85% on-target figure applies only to the one-fifth of impressions Comscore was able to classify. The remaining 80% of impressions contributed no data to the on-target calculation. The campaign may or may not have performed well overall; the CCR data cannot say.

Low measured rates arise for several reasons. Connected TV (CTV) environments have thinner panel coverage than desktop and produce lower measured rates as a result. Publishers that have not integrated their census feed with Comscore fully may show low measured rates even for high-volume placements. Inventory bought through open exchange, where publisher identity is less transparent, can also produce lower measured rates than direct or programmatic guaranteed buys.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The post-campaign summary or presentation cites the on-target percentage without noting the measured rate or the share of impressions it was based on
  • The campaign included significant CTV or open-exchange inventory, both of which produce lower Comscore coverage than desktop direct buys, but the CCR report is presented with no note about coverage variation by environment
  • The team compares on-target percentages across campaigns without normalizing for differences in measured rate between those campaigns
You may want to confirm that every CCR review includes both the on-target percentage and the measured rate, and that any comparison between campaigns or placements notes whether the measured rates were comparable.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a multi-environment campaign spanning desktop display, mobile video, and CTV, the measured rate will likely vary by environment. CTV has lower Comscore panel coverage than desktop. You may want to confirm the CCR report breaks down on-target percentage and measured rate by environment so the two environments are not averaged into a single figure that obscures the CTV coverage gap.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using a mix of direct buys and open-exchange programmatic to reach a professional demographic, measured rate will typically be higher on the direct placements and lower on open-exchange. You may want to confirm the CCR review separates direct and programmatic on-target percentages and notes the measured rate for each so the combined figure is not misread as applying equally to both.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore Campaign Ratings documentation on measured rate (coverage rate) methodology, including how unmeasured impressions are handled and how to interpret coverage variation by environment, is available through Comscore's client portal. Contact your Comscore account representative for the CCR reporting guide.

investigate.intro: Pull the CCR report and confirm whether it shows both the on-target percentage and the measured rate (coverage rate), and whether both figures are included in any summary or presentation of campaign results.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Open the CCR report for the campaign. Locate the column showing the measured rate or coverage rate alongside the on-target percentage. The label may vary by report configuration but represents the share of served impressions that Comscore classified demographically.
Note the measured rate for the campaign overall and, if available, by environment (desktop, mobile, CTV) and by placement or publisher.
Compare the measured rate against the campaign's inventory mix. CTV and open-exchange inventory typically produces lower measured rates than direct-sold desktop or mobile inventory.
Review any post-campaign summary or client-facing presentation to confirm whether both the on-target percentage and the measured rate are included, or whether only the on-target percentage is cited.
If the measured rate is below 30% on a significant portion of the buy, note that the on-target figure applies to a minority of served impressions and should be presented with that context.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the on-target percentage and the measured rate for the campaign. A campaign where both figures are documented and reported together passes. A campaign where only the on-target percentage is documented and the measured rate is not cited in the review, or where the measured rate is below 30% but no context note accompanies the on-target percentage, is a fail, recorded with the on-target percentage, the measured rate, and the share of impressions the on-target figure actually covers.
evaluate.fallback_message: Enter the CCR on-target percentage and the measured rate above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Comscore Campaign Ratings on-target percentage for campaigns on {{site_name}} is reviewed and reported without the accompanying measured rate. An on-target percentage calculated on a low coverage base describes a fraction of served impressions, not the full campaign, and presenting it without that context overstates what the data can confirm. {{voice_recommend}} including the measured rate alongside the on-target percentage in every CCR review and post-campaign summary, noting any environment or placement where the measured rate is low, and avoiding direct comparisons between campaigns where the measured rates differ materially.
